text: The concept of a 'shutdown ritual' — a fixed sequence of actions marking the end of a workday — was formalized by Cal Newport but has roots in older creative practice. Chekhov reportedly cleared his desk and covered it with a cloth each evening; the gesture physically separated work-time from rest. Newport's version involves reviewing incomplete tasks, confirming nothing urgent is unaddressed, then saying aloud a completion phrase. The ritual's purpose is neurological: the brain continues processing unfinished tasks after work ends, a phenomenon Bluma Zeigarnik documented in 1927. The shutdown ritual signals closure, quieting that background loop and protecting recovery time.
question: What phenomenon documented by Bluma Zeigarnik explains why shutdown rituals are neurologically useful?

options.text: The brain suppresses memory consolidation when tasks remain incomplete
The brain continues processing unfinished tasks after work ends
Closing routines activate the parasympathetic nervous system and lower cortisol
Verbal affirmations reinforce positive associations with completed work
